topic.what: Systematic review of the pharmacological properties of Aloe vera, encompassing wound healing, anti-inflammatory effects, gastroprotection, and mucilage-mediated mucosal coating.
topic.how: Comprehensive literature survey synthesizing in-vitro, in-vivo, and available clinical trial evidence for Aloe vera's biological activities across multiple organ systems, with focus on GI applications.
topic.why_matters: Aloe vera's mucilage coating and anti-inflammatory properties offer a mechanistically plausible approach to gastric mucosal protection in gastritis; understanding the evidence base and its limitations guides rational use and future clinical trial design.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Promotion of tissue repair and epithelial regeneration across wound and mucosal contexts
characteristics.value: Well-supported wound healing activity in preclinical and some clinical studies
characteristics.evidence: Multiple in-vitro and in-vivo studies reviewed; some RCTs for cutaneous wounds
characteristics.context: Strongest evidence base for cutaneous wounds; GI mucosal healing extrapolated

characteristics.what_it_measures: Suppression of inflammatory mediators (cytokines, prostaglandins) relevant to gastric mucosal inflammation
characteristics.value: Demonstrated anti-inflammatory activity in preclinical models
characteristics.evidence: In-vitro cytokine suppression assays and animal inflammation models reviewed
characteristics.context: Clinical translation to GI inflammation (gastritis) not directly established

characteristics.what_it_measures: Quality and quantity of evidence supporting GI mucosal protection by Aloe vera
characteristics.value: Preliminary — preclinical evidence exists but clinical trials are limited and methodologically weak
characteristics.evidence: Review identifies gap between promising preclinical results and insufficient clinical RCT data
characteristics.context: Evidence limitation is a central finding of the review

characteristics.what_it_measures: Physical mucosal barrier formation by Aloe vera polysaccharides (acemannan, glucomannans)
characteristics.evidence: Physicochemical characterization of Aloe vera gel polysaccharides
characteristics.context: Gastric pH and enzyme activity may degrade mucilage; in-situ effectiveness uncertain

good_for: Understanding mechanistic rationale for Aloe vera in gastric mucosal protection
Identifying research gaps warranting clinical trials in gastritis
Combining Aloe vera's coating mechanism with anti-H. pylori or anti-inflammatory agents
Ethnopharmacological documentation of traditional GI uses
not_for: Clinical practice guidelines for gastritis treatment based on current evidence
Replacing established gastroprotective therapy (PPIs, sucralfate) without clinical trial support
Standardized dosing recommendations — formulations vary widely
requires: Standardized Aloe vera preparations with defined polysaccharide and aloin content
Adequately powered RCTs in gastritis populations
Aloin removal to avoid laxative effects in oral preparations

novelty: Consolidated multi-system pharmacological evidence for Aloe vera including GI-specific effects
Explicitly acknowledged evidence gap between preclinical promise and clinical validation
Proposed mucilage coating as a distinct, physically-mediated gastroprotective mechanism
open_problems: Clinical RCTs specifically targeting gastritis or peptic ulcer disease with Aloe vera are lacking
Optimal preparation (gel vs whole leaf vs extract), dose, and administration route for GI indications undefined
Stability of active polysaccharides in gastric acid environment not fully characterized
Potential interactions with conventional gastritis medications unstudied
